title: Holy basil adaptogen tea
subtitle: A warming stress-supporting herb infusion for seasonal immunity
excerpt: An infusion of dried holy basil (tulsi) leaf. An Ayurvedic and western herbal stress-supporting herb traditionally taken through the colder months to support general resilience.
sourceNotes: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India, Ocimum tenuiflorum (Ocimum sanctum) reference entry: the stress-supporting herb, rasayana, and immune-supporting tradition. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1, Herba Ocimi Sancti. Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, sweet basil entry, noting the extended Indian and Ayurvedic use of the sacred form.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried holy basil stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh to retain the aromatic oil.

glossaryTerms.definition: An Ayurvedic category of herbs and preparations taken to promote longevity, resilience, and constitutional strength. Holy basil is one of the most important rasayana herbs in the Indian tradition.

content.text: Holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum, tulsi) is a key plant in Ayurvedic tradition. It holds

status: a herb taken as a general tonic to support resilience and wellbeing. In modern western herbal practice it is categorised as an stress-supporting herb. The infusion is pleasant and aromatic: clove-like, warm, slightly spicy from the eugenol and rosmarinic acid content. Taken through the autumn and winter months, once or twice a day, it sits within the traditional use for seasonal immune support and stress resilience.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Blood thinners. Holy basil has mild antiplatelet activity. Check with your GP if you take warfarin or aspirin.

Blood sugar. Holy basil can lower blood sugar. If you take diabetes medicine, watch your blood sugar closely and check with your GP.

Pregnancy. Avoid at medicinal doses in pregnancy. Consult a herbalist or midwife before use in pregnancy.

items.prepNote: dried holy basil (tulsi) leaf; Ocimum tenuiflorum, not sweet basil

content.text: Place the dried holy basil in a teapot with a lid. Pour over the just-boiled water. Put the lid on immediately and steep for 8 minutes. The infusion will be pale amber and smell warmly aromatic and clove-like. Strain into a cup. Add honey if using. Drink warm.

One or two cups (250 ml, containing 2 g dried leaf each) per day, during the autumn and winter months. Take for up to 3 months continuously; take a 4-week break before another course. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve without specialist advice.
Prepare each cup fresh. Dried holy basil stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
On blood thinners without GP approval. On diabetes medicine without blood sugar checks. In pregnancy at medicinal dose.
